race.name: Copper Triangle
race.display_name: Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop
race.tagline: 3 mountain passes. 79 miles. 6,500 feet of climbing — entirely above 9,000 feet. Colorado's definitive high-altitude loop since 2006.

terrain_types: Dedicated Vail Pass bike path (paved, car-free)
High-altitude Alpine roads above 9,000 ft throughout
vitals.start_time: Saturday 6:00 AM – 7:45 AM rolling start. All riders on course by 8:00 AM.
vitals.registration: Online via BikeReg. Cost: ~$130-150 (tiered pricing with early-bird discount). Discounted fee deadline in late February.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Challenge ride — finisher celebration, not competition.
vitals.aid_stations: 5 fully stocked aid stations with hydration drink mix, water, fresh fruit, and sweet/salty snacks. SAG wagons available for mechanical issues and fatigued riders.
vitals.cutoff_time: Course closes at 4:00 PM. If unable to make Vail Pass, sag vehicles available at parking lot beyond East Vail exit.
distance_options: Copper Triangle: 79 miles, ~6,500 ft — Full 3-pass loop (Fremont, Tennessee, Vail)

climate.primary: Colorado Rocky Mountain summer — high-altitude, variable, thunderstorm-prone afternoons
climate.description: Early August in the Colorado high country means cool morning starts at 9,700 ft (45-55F at dawn), warm valley descents (65-75F), and the near-daily threat of afternoon thunderstorms above treeline. The entire route sits above 9,000 ft, so UV exposure is intense and the air is thin. Temperature drops approximately 3.5F per 1,000 feet of elevation gain. The early start is strategic — riders should aim to clear all three passes before the 1:00 PM lightning window opens.
challenges: Afternoon thunderstorms — lightning above 10,000 ft is a genuine safety threat
Entire route above 9,000 ft — relentless altitude with no low-elevation recovery
Temperature swings from cool dawn to warm valleys to cold pass summits
Intense high-altitude UV exposure — 40% stronger than sea level
Low humidity (10-20%) accelerates dehydration at altitude

terrain.primary: Paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths
terrain.surface: Paved mountain highways (CO-91 and US-24) and the paved, car-free Vail Pass Recreation Path. Road surfaces are generally good. The Vail Pass bike path section is entirely separated from vehicle traffic.
features: Fremont Pass (11,318 ft) — the high point, climbing south from Copper Mountain on CO-91 with steady 2.5-3% grades past the Climax Mine
Tennessee Pass (10,424 ft) — climbing northwest on US-24 past historic Camp Hale (10th Mountain Division WWII training site)
Battle Mountain — the unofficial fourth pass between Tennessee Pass and Minturn, a rolling climb through forest
Vail Pass (10,662 ft) — the final climb on the dedicated Vail Pass Recreation Path, car-free with switchbacks near the top
Descent into Leadville valley (9,927 ft) — fast, open descent with views of Colorado's highest peaks
Minturn to Vail — frontage road and bike path along the Eagle River through the Vail Valley

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Colorado's premier high-altitude loop ride since 2006. Three mountain passes entirely above 9,000 ft with $1.5M+ raised for the Davis Phinney Foundation. T2 by score (70). Prestige=3, no override.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Altitude=5 for an entire route above 9,000 ft with Fremont Pass at 11,318 ft — one of the highest cycling events in America. Adventure=4 for three distinct mountain passes, historic Camp Hale, and passage through Leadville and Minturn. Experience=4 for the complete alpine loop narrative. Elevation=3 for 6,500 ft of climbing — significant but moderate by Colorado standards. Technicality=1 (all paved roads and bike paths). cultural_impact=3 for 20 years of Davis Phinney Foundation fundraising and the Camp Hale military heritage connection.

logistics.explanation: Denver International Airport is 90 minutes east on I-70 — straightforward mountain access for a ride that starts above 9,700 feet. The loop format is a major logistical advantage: you finish where you started. No shuttles, no second car, no point-to-point complications. Copper Mountain Resort provides on-site lodging, restaurants, and parking at the start/finish. Frisco and Silverthorne are 10 minutes north with additional hotel and grocery options. Organization has been refined across 20 editions.

length.explanation: 79 miles with roughly 6,500 feet of climbing is a genuine full-day mountain ride, but it's not an ultra-endurance test. Most riders finish in 5-8 hours. The three passes create natural climbing blocks of 30-45 minutes each, separated by descents and valley riding. There's no 'wall' moment — the route distributes its difficulty across the full loop. Challenging for intermediate riders, very manageable for experienced climbers. The altitude is what separates this from a standard 80-mile ride — every mile above 9,000 feet hits different.

technicality.explanation: Fully paved throughout. CO-91 over Fremont Pass and US-24 over Tennessee Pass are standard Colorado mountain highways with good surfaces. The Vail Pass section is on a dedicated paved recreation path with zero vehicle traffic and amusing switchbacks near the top. The descents are fast but straightforward — no hairpin switchbacks or deteriorating surfaces. The challenge is altitude and endurance, not bike handling.

elevation.explanation: 6,500 feet of climbing across three passes in 79 miles is a solid day on the bike. The grades are moderate throughout — Fremont averages 2.5-3%, Tennessee and Vail are similar. No single climb exceeds 30-40 minutes. By Colorado standards, where rides like the Triple Bypass pack 10,800 feet into a single day, the Copper Triangle's climbing is honest but not extreme. What makes the climbing hard is the altitude — climbing at 10,000+ feet with thin air compounds every percent of gradient.

climate.explanation: August in the Colorado high country is generally gorgeous — clear blue mornings, spectacular visibility, comfortable riding temperatures. But the afternoon thunderstorm risk is real and repeats almost daily. Lightning above 10,000 feet is not theoretical. The early 6:00 AM start is designed to get riders over the passes and back to Copper before the storms build around 1:00-2:00 PM. Start on time and ride with purpose, and you'll have a spectacular weather day. Dally past noon on the final pass, and the mountains will remind you who's in charge.

altitude.explanation: This is a top-5 altitude event in American cycling. You start at 9,712 feet — higher than most US cycling events ever reach — and never drop below 9,000 feet for the entire 79 miles. Fremont Pass summits at 11,318 feet. Tennessee Pass at 10,424 feet. Vail Pass at 10,662 feet. Even the valley floor through Leadville sits at 10,152 feet. The cumulative oxygen deficit across three passes above 10,000 feet is the defining challenge. Sea-level riders lose 12-18% of their power at these elevations. Arrive in Colorado at least 2-3 days early.

adventure.explanation: Three mountain passes through three distinct Colorado valleys create a genuine journey. You ride past the Climax Mine on Fremont Pass — once the world's largest molybdenum mine, its terraced slopes visible for miles. You descend into Leadville at 10,152 feet, America's highest incorporated city, where Victorian mining-era architecture lines the main street. You pass the ruins of Camp Hale where the Army's 10th Mountain Division trained for WWII alpine warfare. You roll through tiny Minturn, a working Vail Valley town that the tourists bypass. The final climb over Vail Pass on the car-free bike path delivers panoramic views of the Gore Range. This is not wilderness bikepacking, but it's a complete Colorado experience.

prestige.explanation: The Copper Triangle is a well-known Colorado ride with 20 years of history and strong regional recognition. It's on the bucket list for Front Range cyclists. The Davis Phinney Foundation connection adds gravitas — Davis Phinney is a Boulder-born pro cycling legend. But it hasn't reached the national name recognition of the Triple Bypass, Ride the Rockies, or the eastern equivalents. Outside Colorado, most cyclists haven't heard of it. Solid regional prestige, not yet a national cycling institution.

race_quality.explanation: Two decades of refinement show in the execution. Five well-stocked aid stations across 79 miles means you're never far from support. SAG wagons patrol the course for mechanicals and DNFs. The Alpine Celebration at the finish — beer garden, lunch, live music, cycling expo — is a legitimate post-ride event, not an afterthought. Packet pickup at Burning Stones Plaza in Copper Mountain is efficient. The 2,500-rider cap keeps the course from feeling overcrowded. The Ride Collective (formerly Outside Events) runs a professional operation across their portfolio of Colorado cycling events.

experience.explanation: The Copper Triangle delivers a complete narrative arc. Act One: dawn departure from Copper Mountain, climbing into thin air on CO-91, cresting Fremont Pass at 11,318 feet as the sun illuminates the Tenmile Range. Act Two: the fast descent into Leadville's high valley, the historic mining town awakening as riders roll through. Act Three: the climb over Tennessee Pass past Camp Hale's ghosts, the long forested descent into Minturn. The Finale: the car-free climb over Vail Pass on the recreation path, switchbacks near the top, then the rolling return to Copper Mountain through the Tenmile Canyon. Three distinct mountain environments, three passes, one complete loop.

community.explanation: The Davis Phinney Foundation partnership defines this event's soul. Over $1.5 million raised for Parkinson's research since 2006 — every registration includes an automatic donation. Davis Phinney himself, a Boulder-born pro cyclist diagnosed with Parkinson's at 40, often appears at the event. The Front Range cycling community treats this as a summer tradition. The Alpine Celebration at the finish creates genuine post-ride camaraderie. At 2,500 riders, the field is large enough to feel like an event but small enough that you share the passes with the same faces all day.

field_depth.explanation: Not a race. No timing, no age group results, no podium, no competition. The Copper Triangle is a challenge ride and charity fundraiser. Some riders push for Strava times, but there's no official competitive structure. The field is self-selecting — 2,500 riders willing to climb three passes above 10,000 feet are committed cyclists — but if you need race numbers and results, look elsewhere. The Ride Collective does post ride results by time, but the culture is completion, not competition.

value.explanation: Registration around $130-150 includes five aid stations, SAG support, the Alpine Celebration with beer and food, and a charitable donation to the Davis Phinney Foundation. For a well-organized single-day mountain event with that level of support, it's fair pricing. No custom jersey or gear swag like the Triple Bypass. You're paying for a professionally run day in the mountains with a charitable mission. Reasonable, not exceptional.

expenses.explanation: Summit County in August is peak tourist season. Copper Mountain Resort condos run $200-400/night. Frisco and Silverthorne are somewhat cheaper but still mountain-town pricing. Denver airport is 90 minutes away, so you need at minimum one night of lodging. Food and dining at mountain resort prices. Budget $600-1,200 for a non-local including travel, one or two nights lodging, registration, and meals. The loop format saves the shuttle cost that point-to-point events require. Cheaper than European gran fondos, in line with other Colorado mountain events.

biased_opinion.summary: The Copper Triangle is a masterclass in what altitude does to honest cycling. 79 miles and 6,500 feet of climbing sounds manageable until you register the fine print: the entire route sits above 9,000 feet, the high point reaches 11,318, and you never get a breath of thick air. Three mountain passes — Fremont, Tennessee, Vail — through three Colorado valleys, past Leadville and Camp Hale and Minturn, finishing where you started at Copper Mountain. Twenty years of raising money for the Davis Phinney Foundation. Not the longest ride. Not the most climbing. But an altitude event that quietly exposes the gap between your sea-level fitness and what the Rocky Mountains actually demand.
strengths: Entire route above 9,000 ft — one of America's highest cycling events from start to finish
Loop format eliminates shuttle logistics: start and finish at Copper Mountain Resort
Three distinct mountain passes through Leadville, Camp Hale, and Minturn — genuine Colorado history on two wheels
Davis Phinney Foundation beneficiary — $1.5M+ raised, riding for something beyond personal achievement
Vail Pass bike path section is car-free with switchbacks and Gore Range panoramas
weaknesses: Not competitive: no timing, no awards. A challenge ride, not a race.
Altitude: sea-level riders will suffer disproportionately. Arrive early and acclimatize.
Highway 91 over Fremont Pass carries mining truck traffic — rideable but not idyllic
Peak tourist season pricing in Summit County makes lodging expensive
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want to test what altitude does to your cycling, the Copper Triangle is the answer. Three passes, 79 miles, and not a single pedal stroke below 9,000 feet. The climbing is honest, the scenery is Rocky Mountain spectacular, and the Davis Phinney Foundation mission gives every dollar of your registration meaning. Come for the passes. Stay for the thin air.

final_verdict.one_liner: 3 passes. 79 miles. Never below 9,000 feet. Colorado's premier high-altitude loop.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to experience what genuine high-altitude cycling feels like across three mountain passes in a single day. Yes if you value a well-organized charity ride through Colorado's most historic mountain terrain. Skip if you need race competition or if riding above 10,000 feet is a medical concern.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want more climbing and higher passes: try Triple Bypass. If you want competitive road cycling in the mountains: try Steamboat Gravel. If you want the ultimate Colorado multi-day experience: try Ride the Rockies. If you want a European mountain loop challenge: try La Marmotte.

logistics.airport: Denver International (DEN) — 90 minutes to Copper Mountain via I-70 West. Straightforward mountain highway access. Eagle County Airport (EGE) is 60 minutes west but has limited service.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Copper Mountain Resort is the most convenient option — walk from your condo to the start line at Burning Stones Plaza. Book early: August is peak season. Frisco (10 minutes north on I-70) has more hotel variety and lower prices. Silverthorne and Dillon also offer Summit County lodging. Leadville (on the route, 30 minutes south) has historic hotels and motels at mountain-town prices.
logistics.food: Five aid stations along the course provide hydration, fruit, and snacks. Alpine Celebration at the finish includes lunch and beer garden. Copper Mountain has resort restaurants. Frisco has a walkable main street with restaurants and a brewery. Leadville's Harrison Avenue has restaurants if you're staying the night before.
logistics.packet_pickup: Burning Stones Plaza, Copper Mountain Resort. Pre-ride expo and pickup. Details published by The Ride Collective before the event.
logistics.parking: Copper Mountain Resort parking areas. Arrive early — 2,500 riders fill the lots. The loop format means your car is waiting at the finish.

history.founder: Scott Olmsted, co-founder, in partnership with Copper Mountain Resort and the Davis Phinney Foundation
history.origin_story: The Copper Triangle was conceived to make the most of Copper Mountain's unique position at the intersection of three Colorado mountain passes. The route — a 79-mile loop over Fremont Pass, Tennessee Pass, and Vail Pass — had long been a favorite of local cyclists, and Olmsted formalized it into an organized event in 2006 with the Davis Phinney Foundation as the beneficiary. Davis Phinney, the Boulder-born professional cyclist diagnosed with Parkinson's disease at age 40, lent both his name and his presence to the event. The ride grew from its first edition to 2,500 participants under the Roll Massif banner, then moved to Outside Events in 2022, and was acquired by The Ride Collective in 2023. Twenty years and over $1.5 million in charitable donations later, the Copper Triangle is Colorado's signature high-altitude loop ride.
notable_moments: 2006: Inaugural Copper Triangle ride from Copper Mountain, benefiting the Davis Phinney Foundation for Parkinson's
2015: 10th anniversary edition — cumulative fundraising crosses the $1 million mark for the Davis Phinney Foundation
Since 2006, over $1.5 million raised for Parkinson's research, averaging roughly $140,000 per year
2022: 16th annual edition draws over 2,000 riders; event joins the Outside Events Cycling Series
2023: The Ride Collective acquires Outside Cycling Events, bringing the Copper Triangle into a portfolio of eight professional rides across Colorado and New Mexico
history.reputation: Colorado's premier high-altitude loop ride. The three-pass circuit that Front Range cyclists treat as a summer rite of passage — and visiting riders treat as an altitude reality check.

course_description.character: A three-act alpine loop that never lets you forget where you are. The Copper Triangle traces a triangle through the heart of the Colorado Rockies, connecting three mountain passes through three distinct valleys. You climb past industrial mining heritage on Fremont, descend into America's highest city at Leadville, ascend through WWII military history at Camp Hale, and close the loop on the car-free Vail Pass bike path. The entire route sits above 9,000 feet — there is no low-altitude recovery. The grades are moderate, the road surfaces are good, and the scenery is relentless. What makes this ride hard is not the climbing profile on paper. It's the thin air that makes every gradient feel steeper than it reads.

suffering_zones.label: Copper Mountain — The Dawn Start
suffering_zones.desc: Rolling out of Burning Stones Plaza at 9,712 feet in the cool mountain dawn. The parking lot is buzzing with 2,500 riders. You turn south onto CO-91 and begin climbing immediately. The Tenmile Range towers to the east. Your lungs register the altitude before your legs do.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Copper Mountain Resort, 9,712 ft. Immediate climbing on CO-91.

suffering_zones.label: Fremont Pass — 11,318 ft
suffering_zones.desc: The high point of the day. CO-91 climbs steadily at 2.5-3% past the Climax Mine — the terraced slopes of what was once the world's largest molybdenum mine spread across the mountainside. Mining trucks share the highway. The air gets thin. At 11,318 feet you crest Fremont Pass and the Arkansas River valley opens below, with Leadville's grid visible in the distance. First pass done.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: CO-91, steady 2.5-3% grade, summit at 11,318 ft, mining truck traffic

suffering_zones.label: Leadville — America's Highest City
suffering_zones.desc: You descend into the high valley and roll through Leadville at 10,152 feet — the highest incorporated city in America. Victorian-era mining architecture lines Harrison Avenue. The aid station here is a welcome stop. Refuel well — Tennessee Pass and Battle Mountain await.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Fast descent from Fremont Pass into Leadville valley, 10,152 ft

suffering_zones.label: Tennessee Pass — 10,424 ft
suffering_zones.desc: Heading northwest on US-24, you pass the ruins of Camp Hale — where the Army's 10th Mountain Division trained for alpine warfare in WWII. The ghosts of those soldiers are palpable in the valley. A moderate 4-mile climb with switchbacks brings you over Tennessee Pass at 10,424 feet, with a memorial to the 10th Mountain Division at the summit. The descent through forest, streams, and meadows toward Minturn is one of the ride's great rewards.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: US-24, moderate 4-mile climb with switchbacks, summit at 10,424 ft, Camp Hale historic site

suffering_zones.label: Battle Mountain — The Unofficial Fourth Pass
suffering_zones.desc: Between Tennessee Pass and Minturn, Battle Mountain is the pass nobody warns you about. A rolling climb through dense forest that on fresh legs would barely register. On legs that have already summited two passes above 10,000 feet, every false flat feels like a wall. The descent into Minturn is the release.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling climb on US-24 through forest between Tennessee Pass and Minturn

suffering_zones.label: Minturn — The Quiet Valley Town
suffering_zones.desc: You drop into Minturn, a small working town in the Vail Valley that the ski tourists never see. The Eagle River runs through town. An aid station provides refueling before the final pass. From here the route picks up the frontage road and bike path east toward Vail.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Valley floor, ~7,800 ft, Minturn town, frontage road and bike path

suffering_zones.label: Vail Pass — 10,662 ft
suffering_zones.desc: The final pass. The route transitions to the dedicated Vail Pass Recreation Path — no cars, no trucks, just you and the bike path climbing through the forest. The eastern approach from the Vail side is about 15 miles with steady 3-4% grades and entertaining switchbacks near the top. At 10,662 feet you crest Vail Pass. The Gore Range fills the skyline. Two wheels, three passes, done.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Dedicated paved bike path, 3-4% grades, switchbacks near summit, 10,662 ft

suffering_zones.label: The Return — Descent to Copper Mountain
suffering_zones.desc: Five miles of downhill on the bike path from Vail Pass back to Copper Mountain. The Tenmile Range ahead, the wind in your face, the Alpine Celebration waiting at Burning Stones Plaza. Beer, food, music, and the quiet satisfaction of a three-pass loop at altitude. You're done.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Paved bike path descent, 5 miles, return to Copper Mountain at 9,712 ft

course_description.signature_challenge: The cumulative altitude. No single pass is a leg-breaker. The grades are moderate, the distances between passes are manageable. But the Copper Triangle never lets you below 9,000 feet. By the time you're climbing Vail Pass — your third summit above 10,000 feet — the oxygen deficit has been compounding for 65 miles. The thin air is the opponent, not the gradient.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths
guide_variables.race_weather: Cool dawn start (45-55F at 9,700 ft), warm valleys (65-75F), cold pass summits. Afternoon thunderstorm risk above 10,000 ft.
race_challenges: Entire route above 9,000 ft — sustained high-altitude riding with no low-elevation recovery
Three passes above 10,000 ft compound altitude effects across 79 miles
Afternoon thunderstorms above treeline — start early to clear all passes by early afternoon
Low humidity and intense UV exposure accelerate dehydration and sunburn

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — consecutive threshold efforts of 30-45 minutes
non_negotiables.why: Three passes demand back-to-back climbing blocks. If you don't have mountains, chain together 3-4 sustained efforts at 85-90% FTP with short recovery between to simulate the pass rhythm. The grades are moderate (2.5-4%), so this is a tempo-to-threshold effort at altitude, not a steep climbing grind.

non_negotiables.requirement: Altitude acclimatization or preparation for riding above 9,000 ft for the entire day
non_negotiables.why: The Copper Triangle never drops below 9,000 feet. Fremont Pass at 11,318 ft delivers genuine altitude-induced power loss. Sea-level riders lose 12-18% of their power at the summits. Arrive in Colorado at least 2-3 days early to begin acclimatizing. Increase hydration dramatically — 3-4 quarts of water per day in the high country. Altitude simulation training is beneficial if available.

non_negotiables.requirement: Early start and pacing plan that clears all three passes before 1:00 PM
non_negotiables.why: August afternoon thunderstorms above 10,000 feet are near-daily in the Colorado Rockies. Lightning is the genuine danger. The course closes at 4:00 PM, but the weather window closes earlier. Starting at 6:00 AM and maintaining steady effort through all three passes should put most riders back at Copper Mountain by early afternoon — well ahead of the storms.
